skill.name: context-optimization
skill.description: Guides agents in reducing context usage with compaction, observation masking, cache-friendly prompts, and task partitioning patterns.
skill.summary: Optimize long AI contexts with compaction, masking, caching, and partitioning guidance.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found they are mostly false positives in markdown examples or educational prose. The only confirmed issue is low severity: scripts/compaction.py uses hashlib.md5 to generate short local observation reference IDs, not for authentication, encryption, or exfiltration. No prompt injection, command execution, network calls, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged locations are fenced Python examples or prose, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation. No subprocess, os.system, eval, or command construction evidence was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text shows markdown code fences and explanatory Python snippets. There is no direct shell execution API or user-controlled command path in the flagged regions.

low_findings.title: Static Network Detection Is False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged line discusses KV-cache hit rates and prefix stability. It does not import HTTP libraries, open sockets, call external APIs, or send data over a network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line is educational prose about cache behavior. No network API usage or endpoint is present.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged locations describe context references, cache concepts, or observation storage. They do not enumerate users, hosts, processes, environment variables, filesystems, or network topology.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain context-optimization terminology and local in-memory reference handling. No reconnaissance commands, APIs, or collection logic are present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for the static locations: the flagged documentation and script lines use words such as hash, cache, masking, or stable placeholders. These lines do not perform cryptographic operations or handle secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged static locations were checked in context and do not contain weak algorithm calls. They are terminology matches rather than executable cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Demo Observation Reference IDs
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE, low severity: scripts/compaction.py imports hashlib and uses hashlib.md5 to create an 8-character local observation reference ID. This is unsuitable for security-sensitive identifiers, but it is not used for authentication, encryption, command execution, or network transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of hashlib.md5 use, but semantic context limits impact to local demo reference generation. The code does not expose the value as an access token or secret.

dangerous_patterns.title: Non-Cryptographic Hash Used for Reference ID
dangerous_patterns.description: scripts/compaction.py creates short observation reference IDs with hashlib.md5. Replace this with a random or collision-resistant identifier before using the pattern for sensitive production data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The MD5 call is explicit and easy to verify. The concern is limited because the reference ID is local demo state rather than a credential.

content.value_statement: Long agent sessions lose important details and become expensive. This skill provides compaction, masking, caching, and partitioning patterns for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to compact long context windows based on token utilization thresholds.
Provides observation masking patterns for reducing verbose tool output in active context.
Shows cache-friendly prompt ordering for stable prefixes and better KV-cache reuse.
Describes task partitioning strategies for sub-agents with isolated contexts.
Includes Python utility examples for token estimation, masking, budgeting, and cache metrics.
limitations: Token counting uses rough heuristics and not model-specific tokenizers.
Summarization examples are simple demonstrations, not production-grade compression.
The helper script stores observations in memory and does not persist references.
The skill gives patterns and guidance, not a complete context management platform.

use_cases.title: Plan Long Coding Sessions
use_cases.description: Use compaction and masking rules to preserve decisions while reducing stale tool output.
use_cases.target_user: Software engineers using Codex or Claude Code

use_cases.title: Reduce Agent Runtime Cost
use_cases.description: Apply token budgets and cache-friendly prompt ordering to lower repeated context cost.

use_cases.title: Design Multi-Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Partition large tasks into sub-agents and aggregate concise results into one coordinator context.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Context Pressure
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a growing conversation or agent trace.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this session and identify what is consuming the most context. Recommend one compaction or masking action.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Token Budget
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for a recurring workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a context budget for this workflow. Allocate space for instructions, tools, documents, history, and buffer.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Tool Output Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cleanup for verbose tool-heavy agent runs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these tool outputs. Decide which outputs to keep, summarize, mask, or move to retrievable references.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Partitioned Agent Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture for large tasks that exceed one context window.
prompt_templates.prompt: Split this task into isolated sub-agent contexts. Define each subtask, expected result, aggregation method, and compaction trigger.

output_examples.input: A long coding session has many old test logs, repeated file listings, and current bug details.
output_examples.output: Keep the current bug details and latest decisions. Summarize older test logs and mask repeated listings behind retrievable references.

output_examples.input: A production agent repeats the same system prompt and tool descriptions across many requests.
output_examples.output: Move stable instructions and tool definitions to the front. Put request-specific data later to improve prefix cache reuse.

output_examples.input: A research task requires reviewing many documents before writing a synthesis.
output_examples.output: Assign document review to isolated sub-agents. Return concise findings and merge only validated claims into the coordinator context.

best_practices: Measure token usage before choosing an optimization strategy.
Preserve task goals, user preferences, recent decisions, and unresolved questions.
Test quality after compaction to confirm important details remain available.
anti_patterns: Do not compact system instructions or active task requirements.
Do not mask observations that are still needed for current reasoning.
Do not add optimization machinery before context limits are a real constraint.

faq.question: Does this skill increase a model context window?
faq.answer: No. It helps use the available context more efficiently through compression, masking, caching, and partitioning.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is model-agnostic and applies to agent workflows in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include runnable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Python utility examples for estimating tokens, masking observations, managing budgets, and measuring cache behavior.

faq.question: Is the included token estimator exact?
faq.answer: No. It uses a simple character-based estimate. Production systems should use model-specific tokenizers.

faq.question: When should I use context partitioning?
faq.answer: Use it when one context cannot hold all details or when subtasks can be handled independently.

faq.question: What should never be compacted?
faq.answer: Avoid compacting system instructions, current goals, user preferences, recent decisions, and unresolved blockers.
